units.title: Websites 101
units.description: In this unit, students investigate the architecture of networked digital systems, examining how hardware and software manage, control, and secure data access. They represent online documents through the abstraction of content, structure, and presentation, while developing techniques to acquire, validate, and query structured data within relational databases and spreadsheets. Students implement and debug modular solutions using an object-oriented programming language, validating their algorithms against defined test cases. A focus on cyber security involves developing threat models to identify software vulnerabilities and applying the Australian Privacy Principles to critique digital footprints. Finally, students evaluate their solutions against design criteria, stakeholder user stories, and enterprise opportunities to ensure they meet the needs of a diverse digital landscape.

assessments.title: Web Exam
assessments.description: Students complete a written examination assessing their understanding of networked digital systems and web-based data representation. They investigate how hardware and software components interact to manage, control, and secure access to data within networked environments. Students demonstrate an understanding of how online documents are represented through the separation of content, structure, and presentation, explaining the importance of these abstractions in digital systems. The exam further evaluates their ability to describe the mechanisms used to protect data integrity and maintain security in a web context.

assessments.title: Assessment 2
assessments.description: Students are required to conduct a technical audit and propose a security-focused enhancement for an existing prototype of an online management system. Through this process, students must identify potential vulnerabilities and analyse the system’s data lifecycle, ensuring compliance with the Australian Privacy Principles. The task requires students to model entity relationships within the system’s database, implement and debug modular program code to improve data validation or access controls, and validate these modifications using systematic test cases. Students will document their findings in a technical report that includes a cyber security threat model, an evaluation of the system against design criteria and user stories, and a critical reflection on the digital footprint generated by their proposed enhancements. The final submission must balance technical security requirements with usability, demonstrating a robust understanding of object-oriented programming principles and the ethical responsibilities inherent in managing user data.

units.title: Microbits - Data Gathering and Analysis
units.description: In this unit, students will use micro:bit devices to collect real-world data and investigate how digital systems acquire, store, and process information.

assessments.title: Data Collection
assessments.description: Students will design and implement a micro:bit program to collect sports science–related data.

assessments.title: Wearable Sports Science
assessments.description: Students will design and develop a wearable sports science data collection device using a micro:bit.

units.title: Game Development
units.description: In this unit, students will design, develop, and analyse digital games using emerging game development platforms.

assessments.title: GDevelop Game
assessments.description: Design and develop an original digital game using GDevelop.

assessments.title: Rec Room VR - Game Analyst
assessments.description: Act as gameplay analysts for a VR shooter prototype.
